text: In this video, we present our technique on robotic sigmoidectomy for giant diverticula with the Davinci XI robotic platform. Twenty-nine-year-old female with a history of hypothyroidism was presented with progressive right-sided lower abdominal pain, started a couple of days ago. Abdominal pelvic CT showed non-perforated, inflamed large diverticula in the sigmoid colon, which was classified as Hinche one. with mesenteric fat stranding. Due to location and size of the diverticula, the patient was presented with right-sided abdominal pain. Patient was discharged with antibiotherapy and scheduled for colonoscopy and elective surgery. Colonoscopy was performed six weeks after initial presentation and showed large diverticula in the sigmoid colon.

The patient is positioned in a modified lithotomy position. The surgeon identifies a large diverticula in the sigmoid colon and begins bisection from the medial aspect, proceeding in a medial to lateral fashion. The proximal part of the superior rectal vessels is identified and ligated, while the inferior mesenteric artery and vein are not ligated due to the benign nature of the disease.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection over the Toldus fascia, exposing retroperitoneal structures. Lateral attachments are taken down, and the sigmoid mesocolon is mobilized and dissected over the Toldus fascia along embryological planes. The left ureter, gonadal vessels, and left common iliac artery are identified and preserved. The distal part of the superior rectal vessels is identified, ligated, and divided.

During the lateral dissection, the surgeon exposes and identifies the left ureter. The left mesocolon is mobilized through the taut fascia, and intracorporeal dissection is performed up to the presumed proximal transection site. The upper rectum is transected using a one-fire robotic stapler. Following transection, the colon is exteriorized through the suprapubic incision in preparation for anastomosis.
The surgeon places the anvil and uses a circular 31mm stapler to create an end-to-end colorectal anastomosis. An air leak test is performed, showing no leaks.
As a conclusion, our video demonstrates robotic sigmoidectomy is safe and feasible with new generation robotic platform in a patient with recurrent diverticular disease.
